fact: July 3 is exactly 184 days into the year, which means there are exactly 181 days left - that's almost perfectly balanced!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when fireflies are typically at their flashiest peak, blinking like tiny disco balls in your backyard!
The sun rises super early on July 3 - in some northern places like Alaska, it comes up before 4 AM and doesn't set until after 11 PM!
Baby raccoons born in spring are now old enough to start getting into garbage cans with their moms - July 3 marks peak raccoon mischief season in North America!
This date falls in Cancer season, when the moon's influence on tides is strong and ocean waves can be surprisingly powerful.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, July 3 is deep winter - penguins are huddling together in giant warm piles!
Mosquitoes are usually at their most active around July 3 in northern climates because the hot, humid weather makes them super hungry for blood!
The Earth is actually at its farthest point from the sun around this time of year, even though it feels blazing hot in summer!
Sunflowers in fields are often just starting to turn their giant heads to follow the sun across the sky - like slow-motion solar dancers!
July 3 sunsets often paint the sky in extra-bright oranges and pinks because summer air holds more dust and moisture that scatters light in wild ways!
Cicadas typically emerge from underground around this time after hiding for years, making trees sound like they're buzzing with insect concerts in many parts of North America!
In northern countries, July 3 is when lakes and rivers have usually warmed up enough for fish to become super active and jump out of the water constantly!
